title: Stochastic Differential Equations
motivation: An ordinary differential equation $\dot{x} = b(x)$ describes motion under a deterministic velocity field. A stochastic differential equation adds a fluctuating force of Brownian type, modelling systems — particles in a fluid, asset prices, neuron voltages — whose evolution has both a systematic trend and continuous random shocks.
definition: Given measurable $b \colon \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}^d$ (drift) and $\sigma \colon \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}^{d\times m}$ (diffusion coefficient), a solution of the SDE $dX_t = b(X_t)\,dt + \sigma(X_t)\,dW_t$, $X_0 = x$, is an adapted continuous process satisfying the integral equation $X_t = x + \int_0^t b(X_s)\,ds + \int_0^t \sigma(X_s)\,dW_s$, where the last term is an Itô integral. If $b$ and $\sigma$ are globally Lipschitz with linear growth, a unique strong solution exists (Itô's existence theorem, proved by Picard iteration in $L^2$).
examples: Ornstein–Uhlenbeck: $dX_t = -\theta X_t\,dt + \Sigma\,dW_t$ — mean-reverting, Gaussian, the noise-perturbed linear ODE.
Geometric Brownian motion: $dS_t = \mu S_t\,dt + \Sigma S_t\,dW_t$, solved explicitly by $S_t = S_0 e^{(\mu - \Sigma^2/2)t + \Sigma W_t}$ — the Black–Scholes stock model.
The Bessel SDE $dR_t = \frac{d-1}{2R_t}\,dt + dW_t$ shows how non-Lipschitz coefficients demand finer existence theory.
consequences: Solutions are Markov processes; their transition semigroup is generated by the second-order operator $\mathcal{A} = b \cdot \nabla + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{tr}(\sigma\sigma^{\mathsf{T}} D^2)$.
Weak solutions (where the probability space is part of the unknown) connect SDEs to the martingale problem of Stroock–Varadhan.
Feynman–Kac: expectations of functionals of the solution solve parabolic PDEs driven by $\mathcal{A}$.
